component_identification.content: 4 player boards. 12 starting villagers (3 per player, each showing a house on the back). 18 regular villagers (face-down pile). 6 special villagers (Liquid Woman, Glogo, 2 Cave Cats, 2 Metal Men — each has a cave symbol on the back). 9 key house cards (key symbol). 6 star house cards (star symbol). 25 house cards. 24 outpost cards. 4 starting house cards. 1 reputation board. 25 cave cards. 81 goods tokens: 12 fruit, 12 fish, 12 mushroom (common); 10 rope, 10 clay pot, 10 paper (uncommon); 8 ore, 7 amethyst (rare). 7 dice. 4 wooden cubes (one per player color). 1 encounter book. 1 first-player card. 1 round marker. Coins: 36 ones, 6 fives, 8 tens. 20 potion tokens. 10 cider tokens.

core_game_loop.content: Above and Below is played over exactly 7 rounds. Each round, players take turns assigning villagers from their ready area to perform one action per turn: Explore (send 2+ villagers into a cave for a storybook encounter), Build (use a hammer villager to buy a house or outpost), Harvest (exhaust villagers to collect goods from your buildings), Train (use a quill villager to recruit a new villager from the reputation board), or Labor (exhaust villagers to gain 1 coin each, plus the cider token if you're first to labor this round). You can also perform free actions before your main action: buy goods from another player, put something for sale, or pay 1 coin to refresh a building row. When you have no more villagers or don't want to act, you pass. After all players pass, the round ends: move the round marker, replenish cider, slide remaining villagers on the reputation board left and refill empty slots, rest your villagers using beds/potions/cider, collect income, refresh goods on replenishing buildings, and pass the first-player card left.

detailed_rules.content:
SETUP: Each player gets a player board, 7 coins, 1 starting house card, and 3 starting villagers (placed in the ready area). With 4 players, 2 of your starting villagers (those without a hammer) start in the exhausted area and move to ready at the end of round 1. Place 6 star houses face up in a row. Shuffle 9 key houses, draw 4 face up below the star houses, box the rest. Place the reputation board centrally. Set aside special villagers face up. Shuffle remaining villagers face down, draw 5 onto the reputation board. Round marker on the first cave. Place 1 cider on the reputation board's cider icon. Each player places their colored cube on the torch space of the reputation track. Shuffle house cards and outpost cards into two face-down decks below the reputation board, draw 4 face up from each. Second/third/fourth players each get bonus coins as indicated (2p: second player +1 coin; 3p: second and third +1 coin each; 4p: fourth player +1 coin).

EXPLORE ACTION: Requires at least 2 villagers. Draw the top cave card, place it left of your player board. Slide 2 or more villagers from ready onto the cave card (no upper limit). Roll 1 die and check the cave card's grid to determine which encounter paragraph to read. The player to your left reads the paragraph from the encounter book, including the bold choices and their explore numbers, but NOT the rewards in parentheses. The active player chooses one option aloud. Then roll 1 die per villager on the cave card. Each villager's token shows what rolls give lanterns — for example, a roll of 1+ gives 1 lantern, a roll of 3+ gives 2 lanterns. Place each die on the villager it was rolled for. Total all lanterns. If insufficient, you may EXERT one or more villagers: remove them from the cave card to the injured area on your player board, gaining 1 extra lantern per exerted villager. If your total meets or exceeds at least one explore number for your chosen option, you succeed and gain the rewards from the highest explore number you reached. Remaining villagers on the cave card move to exhausted. Place the cave card to the right of your player board, below your house row. If you fail to meet any explore number, you get nothing — villagers go to exhausted and the cave card goes to the bottom of the cave deck. Some paragraphs have failure penalties.

BUILD ACTION: Requires a villager with a hammer symbol. Slide the hammer villager from ready to exhausted. Pay the coin cost on a house card, star house, key house, or outpost card. Houses/key houses/star houses go in a row next to your starting house. Outposts must be placed on an empty cave card you own (from a previous explore). After building a house or outpost, draw a replacement card so 4 are always available. A villager with a hammer-plus symbol pays 1 fewer coin when building.

HARVEST ACTION: Slide one or more villagers from ready to exhausted. For each villager exhausted, take 1 goods token from one of your houses or outposts. Goods can be kept in your supply, placed for sale, or placed on your advancement track.

TRAIN ACTION: Requires a villager with a quill symbol. Slide the quill villager from ready to exhausted. Pay the coin cost shown on the reputation board for any available villager there. The new villager goes to your exhausted area. Do not refill the reputation board slot until end of round. A villager with a quill-plus symbol pays 1 fewer coin when training.

LABOR ACTION: Slide one or more villagers from ready to exhausted. Gain 1 coin per villager exhausted. The first player to labor each round also gains the cider token from the reputation board.

FREE ACTIONS (before your main action only): Buy From a Player — purchase a good, cider, or potion that another player has placed for sale in their sell area. Minimum price is 3 coins, seller can refuse. Put Something For Sale — place 1 good, cider, or potion in your sell area (top left of player board). Only 1 item for sale at a time, can be swapped only on your turn. Refresh Building Row — pay 1 coin to discard all 4 face-up cards in either the house or outpost row and draw 4 new ones. Once per turn. Cannot refresh key houses or star houses.

ADVANCEMENT TRACK: The row of circles at the bottom of your player board. Place goods left to right with no gaps. If you place a second good of the same type, it stacks on the same slot. Each slot shows two numbers: left = village points per good in that slot at game end; right = your current income level (based on the rightmost occupied slot). Once placed, goods cannot be removed.

REST PHASE (end of round): Spend a potion to move 1 villager from injured to exhausted. Spend a cider to move 1 villager from exhausted to ready. Can use both on the same villager. Then use beds: for each bed symbol on your buildings, move 1 villager from injured to exhausted OR exhausted to ready (not both — a villager can't use 2 beds).

SPECIAL VILLAGERS (obtained only through encounters): Liquid Woman — you lose 1 reputation when she joins. Glogo — pay 1 fewer coin when building with him. Cave Cat — roll a die each time you assign her: 1-2 she refuses (goes to exhausted, action not lost, you may assign another villager); 3-6 she performs the action. Metal Man — has his own built-in bed, cannot use building beds, cannot use cider or potions.

REPUTATION TRACK: Starts on the torch symbol. Gain reputation = move toward bottom of cave (better). Lose reputation = move toward surface (worse). Gained/lost through explore encounters. The track also has small VP symbols at certain positions showing bonus/penalty VP at game end.

scoring_and_endgame.content:
The game ends after exactly 7 rounds (when the round marker cannot move further down). Players count village points from these sources:

1. ADVANCEMENT TRACK: Each good on the track is worth the VP value shown above its slot. Goods stacked in the same slot are each worth that amount. Example: 4 fruit in the third slot at 2 VP each = 8 VP.

2. BUILDINGS: Each building is worth 1 VP. This includes your starting house, all houses, key houses, star houses, and outposts. Empty cave cards (no outpost on them) do NOT count.

3. REPUTATION BONUS: Most reputation = 5 VP. Second most = 3 VP. Third most = 2 VP. In a 2-player game, only the leader gets a bonus (3 VP instead of 5). Ties: add the tied positions' rewards, divide by 2, round up. Example: tied for 1st = (5+3)/2 = 4 VP each. Also check the reputation track for VP bonus/penalty symbols next to your cube's position.

4. CARD BONUSES: Some buildings show bonus VP at the bottom of the card. Some give flat VP. Others give conditional VP — for example, 2 VP per potion you own, or 2 VP per house you own, or 1 VP per empty cave card. Goods must be on the advancement track or in your supply (not on a building) to count for card bonuses.

TIEBREAKER: Most coins wins. Still tied: most villagers. Still tied: most buildings.

IMPORTANT: Coins remaining are NOT worth VP (they only break ties). Unused goods not on the advancement track are NOT worth VP unless a card bonus specifically counts them.

beginner_strategy.content:
1. EXPLORE EARLY AND OFTEN: Cave cards are free to acquire and exploring gives you outpost slots, goods, coins, and reputation. Send 3 villagers instead of 2 when possible — the extra die dramatically increases your chances of hitting higher explore numbers for better rewards. The explore action is the engine that drives everything else.

2. DIVERSIFY YOUR GOODS: The advancement track rewards variety — each new type of good opens a new slot, which increases your income AND the per-good VP value climbs as you fill more slots. Don't stack 5 of the same good in slot 1 (worth 0 VP each). Spread across 4-5 different goods for maximum income and VP.

3. DON'T IGNORE BEDS: Villagers stuck in injured or exhausted can't work. Beds on your buildings are how you recover them. Buy at least one building with beds early — without recovery, you'll run out of usable villagers by round 4-5 and have to waste turns on labor.

4. WATCH THE 7-ROUND CLOCK: The game is shorter than it feels. You only get about 15-20 total actions across the entire game. Don't spend rounds 1-3 purely exploring with no buildings — you need houses and outposts generating goods and income by mid-game. Balance exploration with infrastructure.

5. REPUTATION IS WORTH PURSUING BUT NOT AT ALL COSTS: The 5 VP bonus for most reputation is significant, but losing reputation sometimes gives you better encounter rewards. Don't reflexively pick the 'nice' option in every encounter — weigh the rewards against the reputation cost. If you're already behind on reputation, take the better loot.

metadata.notes: Complete rewrite from official Red Raven Games rulebook PDF. Source: https://cdn.1j1ju.com/medias/9f/b0/4c-above-and-below-rulebook.pdf. All component counts, action descriptions, scoring rules, special villagers, and advancement track mechanics verified against the 16-page official rulebook.
